the-drawer-that-still-sticks: I almost cut the line about lifting the handle and pushing it in first. My editor said nobody would know what that meant without an explanation, and I said that's exactly why it stays — the not-explaining is the point. There are gestures your hand just knows, no manual required, and the song needed at least one detail that pure strangers would have to take on faith. I wrote the bridge last, at two in the morning, and the question 'maybe it opens onto me' scared me enough that I almost deleted it. I left it in because the scared feeling was the most honest thing I'd written all week.
paper-boats-on-the-gutter-river: This one came from an actual homework page, which is a confession I haven't made until now — I tore up a real spelling test of mine to write the second verse, just to remember the texture of the paper folding wrong. I wanted the chorus to feel like it was running out of breath a little, so I kept the lines short and let the rhymes go loose. The hardest decision was the ending: I had a version where the boats made it somewhere, a drain, a river, a sea. I cut all of it. They just go down. That felt truer to how a Tuesday actually disappears.
the-globe-with-the-worn-pacific: I still have the globe. It's the only object from that house I kept, and for years I didn't understand why until I started writing this and the line about the white spot just arrived, already finished, like it had been waiting in the plaster the whole time. I almost made the chorus about wanting to travel — the obvious read — but I rewrote it to be about wanting to want, which is a stranger and smaller thing. The careful thumb in the third verse is true. I really don't spin it anymore. I'm afraid of finishing what the wearing started.
the-attic-stairs-have-fourteen-steps: I built this whole song around a gap I couldn't fix and eventually stopped trying to. Five through nine just wouldn't come back to me no matter how long I sat with it, and my first instinct was to invent something so the count wouldn't have a hole in it. Then I realized the hole was the truest part — memory doesn't lose things evenly, it eats the middle and keeps the edges. So I let the song admit it forgot. The counting structure took longer to arrange than any other song on the record; I wanted it to feel like climbing, not like a list.
the-room-with-the-taped-up-window: This is the song I was most afraid to write, and the only one I wrote completely sober at eleven in the morning, on purpose, because I didn't trust myself with it at night. The decision to draw it 'in pencil, not ink' came from a real argument I had with myself about whether this song belonged on the record at all. I almost left it off the tracklist entirely — some maps you don't want finished. I kept the line 'spring, then summer, then never' exactly as it first came out, unedited, because rewriting it felt like doing to the song what my father did to the window.
the-hallway-that-got-shorter: I actually measured it. Not the real hallway — that house is gone — but I measured a hallway of similar size just to see if nineteen feet sounded believable, because I wanted the specificity to carry weight the feelings alone couldn't. The whole song hinges on the idea that nothing dramatic happened, no slammed doors, and that was the hardest thing to write toward, because drama is easier to sing than absence. I kept cutting verses that explained the family more. The less I explained, the more the hallway did the explaining for me.
letters-pressed-with-flowers-from-the-ya: I wrote the chorus before I let myself write anything else, because I knew if I started with the verses I'd talk myself into reading further into the letters than the song needed. 'I'm not opening this drawer' became a rule I imposed on myself as much as the narrator. The repeated flower was a late fix — originally I had a different bloom in every letter, and it was a friend who pointed out that the sameness was the actual heartbreak. One flower, over and over, from one fence, is so much sadder than variety would have been.
the-empty-chair-at-sunday-dinner: The detail about passing the bread around the gap is the only line in the whole record that made me put the guitar down and walk outside. I wrote it on the first pass and it stayed untouched through every revision, which never happens — usually I rewrite a song eleven times. I made a deliberate choice to never let the narrator say the grandmother's name in the lyric itself, only 'her,' because naming her felt like it would resolve something that the family itself never let resolve. The outro about setting his own table came last, and it surprised me into finishing the song.
the-shed-nobody-was-allowed-to-enter: I wanted this song to feel like it was circling something rather than approaching it, so I kept the chord progression deliberately unresolved — it never quite lands where you expect. The line about hearing him in there at two in the morning, 'not building, not fixing, just standing,' is the closest the album comes to a horror story, and I almost softened it twice. I didn't, because childhood fear doesn't soften things, it sharpens them. Drawing the shed bigger than the house on the atlas was the one purely visual idea I had before I had a single lyric.
the-photograph-with-the-corner-burned-of: I knew the reveal was coming for the whole album before I wrote a word of this song, and that made it terrifying to actually sit down and write, because foreshadowing something for nine tracks means you'd better deliver. I almost made the brother's death more dramatic — a different illness, a different age — before realizing the plainness of 'eleven months, a fever' was what made it unbearable. The line I'm proudest of is 'he's not a hole in the atlas,' because it took four drafts to stop the song from treating him like an absence instead of a person.
the-fever-summer: This is the only song on the record about something I have no memory of at all, which felt like cheating until I realized that's exactly what inheriting grief feels like — you're homesick for a season you never lived in. The casserole dish is real, or real enough; my mother mentioned an unreturned dish once, in passing, years before I ever thought to write about any of this, and it stuck in me like a splinter. I built the whole song backward from that one object, because I trusted the dish more than I trusted my own imagined version of the hospital.
the-name-i-was-never-told: I wrote the name into the lyric before I'd decided whether to keep it, which tells you something about how this song actually happened versus how it's usually described. Saying 'Thomas' out loud in an empty room, alone, while writing this, is the closest the whole album comes to the actual scene it depicts. I repeated the name three times in the bridge because once felt like an announcement and twice felt unfinished — three was the number where it stopped being information and started being a person again. I didn't cry until the second time through.
redrawing-the-floorplan: After thirteen songs of blank rooms and pencil and question marks, this one had to do something structurally different, so I let every chorus reference an object from an earlier song on purpose — the drawer, the letters, the chair. It's the closest thing to a victory lap on the record, and I was nervous it would feel too neat, so I kept the line 'it's not a happier house, it's just a whole one' to argue with my own instinct toward a tidy ending. The hallway gets remeasured in this song deliberately, as a quiet correction to the despair of track six.
the-road-that-was-always-a-circle: I moved four times in the years I was making this record, and somewhere around the third move I noticed I kept ending up facing the same direction on the map without meaning to, which is the entire song in one sentence. The line about keeping the atlas in a box under every new bed is true and slightly embarrassing — I really did that, for over a decade, without once opening the box. I wrote the chorus melody to curve back on itself the way the lyric does, so the music and the idea would be making the same argument at the same time.
the-atlas-closes-on-its-own-front-door: I wrote the ending before I wrote most of the middle of the record, which is unusual for me, but I needed to know where all this was going before I could trust myself to walk through the locked shed and the burned photograph without getting lost. The image of the door left ajar rather than shut came from refusing, twice, to write a tidier final image — closed doors felt like a lie about how memory actually works. The last line, about being glad just to know where the door is, took me longer to earn than any other line on the album, and I mean that literally — it's the last lyric I wrote for the whole project.
